title: Podman fails to run container with userns=keep-id
body:
### Issue Description

I'm trying to use the option `--userns=keep-id` to map my user with the container user, but the container fails to start with the following message:

```
Error: crun: readlink ``: No such file or directory: OCI runtime attempted to invoke a command that was not found
```

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Execute `podman run -d --userns=keep-id --rm ubi9 sleep 100`

### Describe the results you received

The command fails with the following message: 

```Error: crun: readlink ``: No such file or directory: OCI runtime attempted to invoke a command that was not found```

### Describe the results you expected

The container should starts without error and adds an entry for the user into the containers /etc/passwd file.

### podman info output


### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

comments.body:
Hey @Erouan50 

I can't seem to reproduce this on 5.6.2 or tip of main branch.
```shell
❯ podman run --userns=keep-id --rm ubi9 id
uid=1000(ldenny) gid=1000(ldenny) groups=1000(ldenny)

❯ podman run -d --userns=keep-id --rm ubi9 sleep 100
6ae8c9070eea2ef8a7d8836a25531ac2fe55419a79b688068b3685c67c8977ae
```

I'm not sure what the issue could be by maybe there is an issue with the container image cached on your machine? Can you reproduce with other containers? Can you reproduce if you delete the image and pull it again?

Hey @ninja-quokka,

I deleted the whole `~/.local/share/containers/` directory to be sure, and I still have the same issue with this image or any other one:

```
~> podman run --userns=keep-id --rm ubi9 id
Resolved "ubi9" as an alias (/etc/containers/registries.conf.d/000-shortnames.conf)
Trying to pull registry.access.redhat.com/ubi9:latest...
Getting image source signatures
Checking if image destination supports signatures
Copying blob d9ce76bfbf66 done   |
Copying config d60669a3ae done   |
Writing manifest to image destination
Storing signatures
Error: crun: readlink ``: No such file or directory: OCI runtime attempted to invoke a command that was not found
```
Debian:
```
~> podman run --userns=keep-id --rm debian id
Resolved "debian" as an alias (/etc/containers/registries.conf.d/000-shortnames.conf)
Trying to pull docker.io/library/debian:latest...
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob 2981f7e8980b done   |
Copying config 910a89191f done   |
Writing manifest to image destination
Error: crun: readlink ``: No such file or directory: OCI runtime attempted to invoke a command that was not found
```
**Edit:** I quickly created a virtual machine Fedora 42 and install podman on it, no issue... There is definitely something wrong on my laptop. I'm trying to run crun manually to check the problem, but I'm lost. Do you have a step by step procedure that I could try?

Hi @Erouan50 

You can try running with debug logging to see any any hints are printed: `--log-level=debug`

You can try using runc rather than crun to see if you can isolate the issue to crun: `--runtime runc`

If you can, you could try running [podman system reset](https://docs.podman.io/en/stable/markdown/podman-system-reset.1.html)  - note this will remove any Podman you have created.

If that doesn't help I would try reinstalling Podman and report back :)

> You can try using runc rather than crun to see if you can isolate the issue to crun: --runtime runc

I tried (after exec `podman system reset`), but I get a permission denied:

```
~> podman --runtime runc run --userns=keep-id --rm ubi9  id
Resolved "ubi9" as an alias (/etc/containers/registries.conf.d/000-shortnames.conf)
Trying to pull registry.access.redhat.com/ubi9:latest...
Getting image source signatures
Checking if image destination supports signatures
Copying blob 34b5c851d9cf done   |
Copying config d73ae4233d done   |
Writing manifest to image destination
Storing signatures
Error: runc: runc create failed: unable to start container process: error during container init: error preparing rootfs: remount-private dst=/home/antoine.rouaze/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay/bb0d46aa7970905a92d5fa33b869dca01fbd55bcf26066d1e351675a43ff320a/merged, flags=MS_PRIVATE: permission denied: OCI permission denied
```

> If you can, you could try running [podman system reset](https://docs.podman.io/en/stable/markdown/podman-system-reset.1.html) - note this will remove any Podman you have created.

Is still have the same issue.

> If that doesn't help I would try reinstalling Podman and report back :)

I uninstall podman with following command, and re-install it and I still have the same issue:

```
sudo dnf remove podman crun runc
```

Thank you for your help!

I had the same issue (the `Error: crun: readlink ``: No such file or directory: OCI runtime attempted to invoke a command that was not found` with keep-id) the problem seems to be the use of  `--root --runroot --tmpdir` tags...
Using podman 5.7.1 on archlinux.

@Erouan50 

Could you please try again with overriding the containers entrypoint?

```
podman run --log-level=debug --userns=keep-id --rm --entrypoint id ubi9
```

@ninja-quokka I have the same problem, here's my output from that.
<details>
  <summary>Click to expand</summary>

```
[bkhs@fedora ~]$ podman run --log-level=debug --userns=keep-id --rm --entrypoint id ubi9
INFO[0000] podman filtering at log level debug
```  

PS hi!

Reproduced on Fedora 43 with both packaged and upstream builds.

**Root cause analysis:**

The problem is `0700` home directory permissions (Fedora default, set via `HOME_MODE 0700` in `/etc/login.defs`). With `--userns=keep-id`, container UID 0 maps to a subordinate host UID (e.g., 100001) that can't traverse the user's home directory to reach the container rootfs under `~/.local/share/containers/storage/`.

Strace of the crun process inside the user namespace shows the exact failure in `resolve_rootfs_path()`:
```
readlink("/home", ...)                  = -1 EINVAL (Invalid argument)
readlink("/home/test_user", ...)        = -1 EINVAL (Invalid argument)
readlink("/home/test_user/.local", ...) = -1 EACCES (Permission denied)
readlinkat(15, "", ...)                 = -1 ENOENT (No such file or directory)
```

1. `realpath(def->root->path)` walks the path components. The first two return EINVAL (not symlinks — normal). The third fails with EACCES because the mapped UID can't traverse the `0700` home directory.
2. The fallback calls `safe_readlinkat(fd, "", ...)` which also fails with ENOENT.
3. The error is reported as `readlink ''` — the empty string is the `name` parameter passed to the error formatter, not the actual path being readlinked.

The problematic code in crun `src/libcrun/container.c` `resolve_rootfs_path()`:
```c
  *rootfs = realpath (def->root->path, NULL);
  if (UNLIKELY (*rootfs == NULL))
    {
      /* If realpath failed for any reason, try the relative directory.  */
      if (def->root->path[0] == '/')
        {
          ...
          len = safe_readlinkat (ret, "", &cwd, 0, err);
```

**Reproduced with upstream builds:**
- podman 6.0.0-dev (`7534429`)
- crun main (`347750a`)
- Kernel 6.19.11-200.fc43.x86_64, btrfs

**Workaround:** `chmod 701 ~`

**Impact on toolbox:** Toolbox hardcodes `--userns=keep-id` for non-root users, so this completely breaks `toolbox enter` on default Fedora installs. The toolbox error is even less informative: `Error: failed to start container fedora-toolbox-43`.

Additionally, the current error message `crun: readlink '': No such file or directory` is practically useless for diagnosing this problem. It would help a lot if crun or podman could detect the permission issue on the rootfs path and report it clearly, e.g. mentioning that the home directory permissions prevent the mapped user namespace UIDs from accessing the container storage.

Still reproducible on newer versions than the OP — podman **5.8.2** + crun **1.27.1** (commit `3ec076b3b6714ec2f1a10533cf18d5605a6de637`) — so at least here it wasn't fixed by updates.

The trigger is the **keep-id target UID — not the image or the command**. Minimal repro on stock `alpine:latest`:

```console
$ podman run --rm --userns=keep-id docker.io/library/alpine:latest true
Error: crun: readlink ``: No such file or directory: OCI runtime attempted to invoke a command that was not found

$ podman run --rm --userns=keep-id:uid=0,gid=0 docker.io/library/alpine:latest true
$ echo $?
0
```

Same behavior on `ubuntu:latest` and `mcr.microsoft.com/devcontainers/base:ubuntu`:

| `--userns` | result |
| --- | --- |
| `keep-id` (host UID → same UID) | ✗ `readlink ''` |
| `keep-id:uid=1000,gid=1000` | ✗ `readlink ''` |
| `keep-id:uid=0,gid=0` | ✓ works |
| *(no keep-id)* | ✓ works |

crun only succeeds when the host user maps to container **UID 0**; any non-zero target UID fails. The executable is irrelevant (`true`, `/bin/true`, `/usr/bin/true` all fail identically), so it's during container init — not exec / PATH resolution.

Extra data points:
- **Appears crun-specific** — running the same containers with `--runtime runc` does not produce the `readlink` error.
- **Not ID-range exhaustion** — `/etc/subuid` is `165536:65536` (65536 IDs); exhaustion produces a different `not enough unused IDs` error.
- **Storage location isn't the factor** — `uid=0` works on the exact same store that fails with the default mapping.

**Correction to my earlier comment — I got two key things wrong, apologies.**

I previously reported this as crun-specific (runc unaffected) and said storage location wasn't a factor. After a much longer investigation both claims turned out to be wrong, and my case appears to be **environment-specific**, so it may not be the same root cause as the OP's. Setting the record straight:

**1. Not crun-specific.** runc fails on the exact same setup, just with a different surface error:
```
runc: ... error preparing rootfs: remount-private dst=.../overlay/<hash>/merged, flags=MS_PRIVATE: permission denied
```
crun says `readlink ''`, runc says `MS_PRIVATE` — but they fail and succeed *together*. So this isn't a crun bug.

**2. It was storage layout/permissions, not the runtime or the UID.** On my setup (NixOS, rootless, btrfs, with a *relocated* store because `$HOME` is `0700`), the keep-id RUN failed for both runtimes, and what actually determined success was *where the graph root lived*:
- a store under `/var/lib/containers/...` failed even with a world-writable parent;
- a store whose entire ancestry was `≤0755` failed;
- a store **off** `/var/lib/containers` under a **`1777`** (sticky, world-writable) parent — the `/tmp` model — worked, for **both** crun and runc.

The `--userns=keep-id:uid=0` "workaround" several of us noted only worked because of this — it's a symptom, not the cause.

I couldn't tie this to a specific podman/crun/kernel change, and since keep-id + the default store works fine for the vast majority, **my failure is almost certainly something environment-specific** (NixOS / btrfs / the `0700` home / my relocation) rather than the OP's default-store scenario. So please treat this as an extra data point + a heads-up, not a diagnosis of #27785.

Two things that might still help triage here:
- **Try the other runtime** before concluding "crun bug" — if runc also fails (`MS_PRIVATE`), the runtime isn't the cause.
- **The graph root's path/permissions matter** — relocating it (e.g. under a `1777` parent like `/tmp`) is worth trying as a diagnostic.

Thanks for taking a look at this @wdanilo

Until we have a solid reproducer this issue is blocked.

I think I found a reproducer. I can trigger this issue, when `~/.local/share` is not owned by the user group (gid=1000) but by some other group.

## Reproducer

Assume the user `phoenix (uid=1000, gid=1000)` and the following `Containerfile`:

```
FROM registry.opensuse.org/opensuse/tumbleweed
USER 1000:1000
CMD /bin/bash
```

Building the container and running it with default configuration works fine:

```
$ podman build . -t hase-hopp
$ podman run --rm --userns=keep-id -ti hase-hopp id
uid=1000(phoenix) gid=1000(phoenix) groups=1000(phoenix)
```

Now, if I do a `podman system reset` and change the group of `~/.local.share` to e.g. the group `users (gid=100)` then I can reproduce the issue:

```
$ podman system reset
$ rm -rf .local/share/containers      # ensure there are no remnant directories
$ sudo chgrp -R users /home/phoenix/.local/share

$ podman build . -t hase-hopp
$ podman run --rm --userns=keep-id -ti hase-hopp id
Error: container create failed (no logs from conmon): conmon bytes "": readObjectStart: expect { or n, but found , error found in #0 byte of ...||..., bigger context ...||...
$ podman run --rm --runtime crun --userns=keep-id -ti hase-hopp id
Error: crun: readlink ``: No such file or directory: OCI runtime attempted to invoke a command that was not found
```

So it looks like the `userns=keep-id` stumbles, when the container directories are not owned by the same gid.

I believe I had a user run into this today on EL8 and I was able to reproduce it on fully updated EL8 and Fedora 43. 

My simple test case it just changing ~/ group ownership:

```
[testing@podman ~]$ id 
uid=1001(testing) gid=1002(other) groups=1002(other),1001(testing) context=unconfined_u:unconfined_r:unconfined_t:s0-s0:c0.c1023
[testing@podman ~]$ ls -ld ~testing
drwx------. 5 testing testing 105 Jul 13 21:26 /home/testing
[testing@podman ~]$ podman run --userns=keep-id --rm hello-world
Error: crun: readlink ``: No such file or directory: OCI runtime attempted to invoke a command that was not found
[testing@podman ~]$ chgrp other ~
[testing@podman ~]$ podman run --userns=keep-id --rm hello-world
!... Hello Podman World ...!
...
```
       
Is this the same scenario as the other cases from above changing `~/.local/` data?

The error was mildly different on EL8  `OCI permission denied` vs the above.  I think that's just crun vs non-crun on EL8.
